title: Number Systems: Binary and Hexadecimal
summary: Convert between decimal, binary, and hexadecimal, and understand why computers use these systems.
content_html:
Positional Number Systems

We use positional notation - the further left a digit is, the greater the power of its base it is multiplied by. This same system works for any radix (base).

Decimal (base-10): 348 = 3x10^2 + 4x10^1 + 8x10^0
Binary  (base-2):  Digits 0 and 1
Octal   (base-8):  Digits 0-7
Hex     (base-16): Digits 0-9, A-F

Binary Numbers

Computers work in binary because it maps directly to two electronic states (low/high voltage). Each bit position represents a power of 2:

Bit weights (8-bit): 128  64  32  16  8  4  2  1
                      2^7 2^6 2^5 2^4 2^3 2^2 2^1 2^0

Example: 01001110
  = 0x128 + 1x64 + 0x32 + 0x16 + 1x8 + 1x4 + 1x2 + 0x1
  = 64 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 78

Binary Counting

Dec  Binary    Dec  Binary
  0  0000        8  1000
  1  0001        9  1001
  2  0010       10  1010
  3  0011       11  1011
  4  0100       12  1100
  5  0101       13  1101
  6  0110       14  1110
  7  0111       15  1111
                16  10000

Decimal to Binary Conversion

Repeatedly divide by 2, record remainders from bottom to top:

Convert 78 to binary:
  78 / 2 = 39 remainder 0
  39 / 2 = 19 remainder 1
  19 / 2 =  9 remainder 1
   9 / 2 =  4 remainder 1
   4 / 2 =  2 remainder 0
   2 / 2 =  1 remainder 0
   1 / 2 =  0 remainder 1  (MSB)
  Read bottom-up: 1001110

Hexadecimal Numbers

Hex is a compact shorthand for binary. One hex digit = exactly 4 binary bits (one nibble). Conversion is done one digit at a time.

Hex  Dec  Binary
  0    0  0000
  1    1  0001
  ...  ...  ...
  9    9  1001
  A   10  1010
  B   11  1011
  C   12  1100
  D   13  1101
  E   14  1110
  F   15  1111

Hex to Binary Conversion

4E hex = 0100 1110 binary
  4 = 0100
  E = 1110

0xFF = 1111 1111 = 255 (max value of 1 byte)

Bitwise Operations

Individual bits can be manipulated using Boolean operations - fundamental to how computers process data:

NOT: invert each bit
  NOT 1010 = 0101

AND: 1 only when both bits are 1
  1010 AND 1100 = 1000

OR:  1 when either bit is 1
  1010 OR  1100 = 1110

XOR: 1 when bits are different
  1010 XOR 1100 = 0110

Notation Reference

Binary:       0b01110  or  %01110
Hexadecimal:  0x1E     or  1Eh
Decimal:      no prefix (default)
